content.content:
580	for the full design and [per-step record](./notes/chores-05.md#per-step-record)
581	for what each `0.37.0-N` dev step shipped.
582	
583	### Testing push + finalize
584	
585	Always test against a throwaway fixture, never the live workspace.
586	Scaffold one with `vc-x1 init --repo-local <PARENT>` (no GitHub, no
587	network), then run the complete push + finalize flow end-to-end.
588	The code repo uses plain `jj git push`; the session repo uses
589	`vc-x1 finalize` to squash trailing writes and push in one shot.
590	
591	`--repo-local` lays out:
592	```
593	<PARENT>/
594	  remote-code.git/     bare git remote for code repo
595	  remote-claude.git/   bare git remote for .claude session repo
596	  <NAME>/              code repo (jj colocated, main tracks origin)
597	    .vc-config.toml    path="/",       other-repo=".claude"
598	    .gitignore         /.claude /.git /.jj /target /.vc-x1
599	    .claude/           session repo (jj colocated, main tracks origin)
600	      .vc-config.toml  path="/.claude", other-repo=".."
601	      .gitignore       .git .jj
602	```
603	
604	```bash
605	parent=$(mktemp -u /tmp/vc-x1-test-XXXXXX)
606	vc-x1 init work --repo-local "$parent"
607	work="$parent/work"
608	session="$parent/work/.claude"
609	
610	# 1. code repo: described commit → advance main → push
611	echo hello > "$work/hello.txt"
612	jj describe @ -R "$work" -m 'feat: add hello.txt'
613	jj bookmark set main -r @ -R "$work"
614	jj git push -R "$work"
615	
616	# 2. session repo: trailing writes → finalize (squash into @-, push)
617	echo notes > "$session/notes.md"
618	vc-x1 finalize --repo "$session" --squash --push main --detach \
619	    --log "$session/finalize.log"
620	
621	# 3. inspect the detached child's log once it's done (≈10s by default)
622	sleep 12 && cat "$session/finalize.log"
623	
624	# 4. cleanup when done
625	rm -rf "$parent"
626	```
627	
628	**Why `jj git push` for code but `finalize` for `.claude`?** The
629	code repo's workflow is a plain dev commit on `@-` that we push
630	directly. The session repo mirrors the bot's runtime pattern:
631	session writes land in `@` (above the last committed dev commit),
632	and `finalize --squash @,@-` folds those trailing writes into the
633	dev commit just before pushing, so one atomic state goes upstream.
634	
635	The log file shows timestamped (nanoseconds) entries with PIDs,
636	covering the full flow: `main` entry/exit, `finalize` entry/exit,
637	`detach` spawn, and `finalize_exec` in the child process.
638	
639	Example — detached finalize against a fresh fixture. What the user
640	sees in the terminal (the parent process) is the pre-flight plan and
641	the detach confirmation; the child's work continues in the background:
642	```
643	$ vc-x1 finalize --repo "$session" --squash --push main \
644	    --detach --delay 1 --log "$session/finalize.log"
645	finalize: squash @ → @- in /tmp/vc-x1-test-8PD4x8/work/.claude
646	finalize: set bookmark 'main' mwutluxv 31a49010 → mwutluxv 31a49010 (@-)
647	finalize: push 'main' to remote
648	finalize: detached (pid 103787), log: /tmp/vc-x1-test-8PD4x8/finalize.log
649	```
650	
651	A few seconds later the log file (authoritative when the caller
652	closes the child's pipes) shows the full run, including the child's
653	own squash/push output:
654	```
655	$ cat "$session/finalize.log"
656	[INFO ] vc_x1::finalize: finalize: squash @ → @- in /tmp/vc-x1-test-8PD4x8/work/.claude
657	[INFO ] vc_x1::finalize: finalize: set bookmark 'main' mwutluxv 31a49010 → mwutluxv 31a49010 (@-)
658	[INFO ] vc_x1::finalize: finalize: push 'main' to remote
659	[INFO ] vc_x1::finalize: finalize: detached (pid 103787), log: /tmp/vc-x1-test-8PD4x8/finalize.log
660	[INFO ] vc_x1::common: Working copy  (@) now at: ovumtpup fa1cb861 (empty) (no description set)
661	Parent commit (@-)      : mwutluxv 584571ba main* | initial commit
662	[INFO ] vc_x1::common: Nothing changed.
663	[INFO ] vc_x1::common: Changes to push to origin:
664	  Move sideways bookmark main from 31a490102db7 to 584571ba54af
665	```
666	
667	Pre-flight failures (bookmark missing, non-tracking remote, squash
668	revset unresolved, push target lacks a description) exit the parent
669	synchronously with a non-zero status and a pointed error on stderr,
670	before the child is ever spawned.
671	
672	## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers
673	
674	Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)

file.content:
for the full design and [per-step record](./notes/chores-05.md#per-step-record)
for what each `0.37.0-N` dev step shipped.

### Testing push + finalize

Always test against a throwaway fixture, never the live workspace.
Scaffold one with `vc-x1 init --repo-local <PARENT>` (no GitHub, no
network), then run the complete push + finalize flow end-to-end.
The code repo uses plain `jj git push`; the session repo uses
`vc-x1 finalize` to squash trailing writes and push in one shot.

`--repo-local` lays out:
```
<PARENT>/
  remote-code.git/     bare git remote for code repo
  remote-claude.git/   bare git remote for .claude session repo
  <NAME>/              code repo (jj colocated, main tracks origin)
    .vc-config.toml    path="/",       other-repo=".claude"
    .gitignore         /.claude /.git /.jj /target /.vc-x1
    .claude/           session repo (jj colocated, main tracks origin)
      .vc-config.toml  path="/.claude", other-repo=".."
      .gitignore       .git .jj
```

```bash
parent=$(mktemp -u /tmp/vc-x1-test-XXXXXX)
vc-x1 init work --repo-local "$parent"
work="$parent/work"
session="$parent/work/.claude"

# 1. code repo: described commit → advance main → push
echo hello > "$work/hello.txt"
jj describe @ -R "$work" -m 'feat: add hello.txt'
jj bookmark set main -r @ -R "$work"
jj git push -R "$work"

# 2. session repo: trailing writes → finalize (squash into @-, push)
echo notes > "$session/notes.md"
vc-x1 finalize --repo "$session" --squash --push main --detach \
    --log "$session/finalize.log"

# 3. inspect the detached child's log once it's done (≈10s by default)
sleep 12 && cat "$session/finalize.log"

# 4. cleanup when done
rm -rf "$parent"
```

**Why `jj git push` for code but `finalize` for `.claude`?** The
code repo's workflow is a plain dev commit on `@-` that we push
directly. The session repo mirrors the bot's runtime pattern:
session writes land in `@` (above the last committed dev commit),
and `finalize --squash @,@-` folds those trailing writes into the
dev commit just before pushing, so one atomic state goes upstream.

The log file shows timestamped (nanoseconds) entries with PIDs,
covering the full flow: `main` entry/exit, `finalize` entry/exit,
`detach` spawn, and `finalize_exec` in the child process.

Example — detached finalize against a fresh fixture. What the user
sees in the terminal (the parent process) is the pre-flight plan and
the detach confirmation; the child's work continues in the background:
```
$ vc-x1 finalize --repo "$session" --squash --push main \
    --detach --delay 1 --log "$session/finalize.log"
finalize: squash @ → @- in /tmp/vc-x1-test-8PD4x8/work/.claude
finalize: set bookmark 'main' mwutluxv 31a49010 → mwutluxv 31a49010 (@-)
finalize: push 'main' to remote
finalize: detached (pid 103787), log: /tmp/vc-x1-test-8PD4x8/finalize.log
```

A few seconds later the log file (authoritative when the caller
closes the child's pipes) shows the full run, including the child's
own squash/push output:
```
$ cat "$session/finalize.log"
[INFO ] vc_x1::finalize: finalize: squash @ → @- in /tmp/vc-x1-test-8PD4x8/work/.claude
[INFO ] vc_x1::finalize: finalize: set bookmark 'main' mwutluxv 31a49010 → mwutluxv 31a49010 (@-)
[INFO ] vc_x1::finalize: finalize: push 'main' to remote
[INFO ] vc_x1::finalize: finalize: detached (pid 103787), log: /tmp/vc-x1-test-8PD4x8/finalize.log
[INFO ] vc_x1::common: Working copy  (@) now at: ovumtpup fa1cb861 (empty) (no description set)
Parent commit (@-)      : mwutluxv 584571ba main* | initial commit
[INFO ] vc_x1::common: Nothing changed.
[INFO ] vc_x1::common: Changes to push to origin:
  Move sideways bookmark main from 31a490102db7 to 584571ba54af
```

Pre-flight failures (bookmark missing, non-tracking remote, squash
revset unresolved, push target lacks a description) exit the parent
synchronously with a non-zero status and a pointed error on stderr,
before the child is ever spawned.

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)

input.old_string:
## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md):
input.new_string:
## Testing

The repo ships two flavors of tests:

- **In-process tests** — `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { … }` blocks inside
  `src/*.rs`. These call library code directly (no subprocess spawn)
  and run fastest. The dual / POR fixture tests under
  `src/init.rs::tests` build throwaway workspaces by invoking
  `init::init_with_symlink` as a function.
- **CLI subprocess integration tests** — files under `tests/`. These
  spawn the `vc-x1` binary that Cargo built (via
  `env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1")`) so the full argument-parsing,
  exit-code, and stdout/stderr surface is exercised. Each subprocess
  gets a `HOME` override so the user's real `~/.config/vc-x1/` can't
  leak in or get clobbered.

Run everything with:

```bash
cargo test                 # unit + integration
cargo test --lib           # in-process only
cargo test --test cli_init # one integration test crate
```

### Test tempdir location

Both test layers create throwaway fixtures under a tempdir. The
parent directory resolves in priority order:

1. `$VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR` — explicit env override.
2. `std::env::temp_dir()` — standard fallback (`$TMPDIR` on Unix,
   else `/tmp`).

Useful when you want tests on a tmpfs / SSD / project-local path
without exporting `TMPDIR` globally:

```bash
VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR=/dev/shm/vc-x1 cargo test
```

Fixture directories are named `vc-x1-test-<tag>-<ts>-<n>` (in-process)
and `vc-x1-cli-test-<tag>-<ts>-<n>` (CLI). RAII drop removes them on
test exit; SIGKILLs / panics in `Drop` can leak — search and clean
manually:

```bash
find "${VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR:-${TMPDIR:-/tmp}}" -maxdepth 1 \
     -name 'vc-x1-*test*' -mtime +1 -exec rm -rf {} +
```

A future enhancement (tracked in `notes/todo.md`) extends the
priority chain through `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` and project-local
`.vc-config.toml`.

canonical in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md):

## Testing

The repo ships two flavors of tests:

- **In-process tests** — `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { … }` blocks inside
  `src/*.rs`. These call library code directly (no subprocess spawn)
  and run fastest. The dual / POR fixture tests under
  `src/init.rs::tests` build throwaway workspaces by invoking
  `init::init_with_symlink` as a function.
- **CLI subprocess integration tests** — files under `tests/`. These
  spawn the `vc-x1` binary that Cargo built (via
  `env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1")`) so the full argument-parsing,
  exit-code, and stdout/stderr surface is exercised. Each subprocess
  gets a `HOME` override so the user's real `~/.config/vc-x1/` can't
  leak in or get clobbered.

Run everything with:

```bash

cargo test --lib           # in-process only
cargo test --test cli_init # one integration test crate
```

Both test layers create throwaway fixtures under a tempdir. The
parent directory resolves in priority order:

1. `$VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR` — explicit env override.
2. `std::env::temp_dir()` — standard fallback (`$TMPDIR` on Unix,

Useful when you want tests on a tmpfs / SSD / project-local path
without exporting `TMPDIR` globally:

Fixture directories are named `vc-x1-test-<tag>-<ts>-<n>` (in-process)

test exit; SIGKILLs / panics in `Drop` can leak — search and clean

message.content: So from vc-x1/tests/common/mod.rs::some_test calling resove_tmp_root() it can find resolve_tmp_root() from where some_test is using path = ../../src/test_tmp_root.rs

content.text:
Yes, exactly. The resolution chain:

1. **`tests/cli_init.rs`** has `mod common;` → loads `tests/common/mod.rs`.
2. **`tests/common/mod.rs`** has `#[path = "../../src/test_tmp_root.rs"] mod test_tmp_root;` → loads the shared file.
3. **`#[path]` is relative to the directory of the file containing the `mod` declaration**, which is `tests/common/`. So:
   - `../` → `tests/`
   - `../../` → project root
   - `../../src/test_tmp_root.rs` → `<project>/src/test_tmp_root.rs` ✓

After the load, `tests/common/mod.rs` has a sub-module named `test_tmp_root`, and `use test_tmp_root::resolve_tmp_root;` brings the function into the `common` namespace. Then `unique_base` calls it like any local function.

A few subtle properties worth noting:

- **Two separate compilations of the same file.** The integration-test crate compiles `src/test_tmp_root.rs` independently from the binary's compilation. Two object files, same source. They never link to each other — each crate gets its own private copy. (That's why the file must be `pure stdlib` — if it referenced `crate::config::…`, the integration-test crate would fail to resolve the path because *its* crate root is `tests/cli_init.rs`, not `src/main.rs`.)

- **`#[path]` doesn't mean "look up the symbol in another crate."** It only redirects where Rust searches for the *file* backing a module declaration. The integration-test crate doesn't know about `vc-x1`'s binary modules — it just compiles a file that happens to live under `src/`.

- **Editor / `rust-analyzer`** sometimes gets confused about which crate context to use when navigating a `#[path]`-included file. If you "go to definition" from `tests/common/mod.rs`, it usually works, but `rust-analyzer` may show the file as belonging to the binary crate first — that's a tooling artifact, not a compile-time concern.

input.old_string: ### Decisions made during design
input.new_string:
### CLI subprocess integration tests + tempdir-root sharing (0.41.1-6.4)

First true CLI integration tests for the project. Adds a `tests/`
crate that spawns the `vc-x1` binary that Cargo built (via
`env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1")`) so argument parsing, exit codes, and
stdout/stderr are exercised end-to-end — distinct from the
in-process fixture tests under `src/init.rs::tests`, which call
`init::init_with_symlink` as a Rust function.

This step bumps the test-strategy floor from "in-process only" to
"both layers"; it doesn't yet retire any in-process tests. The
in-process tests stay as fast unit-ish coverage; subprocess tests
are added as state-mutating cases warrant a real binary spawn.

**New harness — `tests/common/mod.rs`:**

- `vc_x1() -> Command` — `Command::new(env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1"))`.
- `run_ok` / `run_err` — wrap `Command::output`, panic with
  stdout+stderr embedded if exit status doesn't match expectation.
- `CliFixture` — RAII tempdir owner. Each subprocess invocation
  gets `HOME` overridden to `<base>/home/` so the user's real
  `~/.config/vc-x1/` can't leak in or get clobbered.
- `unique_base(tag)` — mirrors `src/test_helpers::unique_base` but
  uses a `vc-x1-cli-test-` prefix to distinguish from in-process
  fixtures' `vc-x1-test-` paths.
- Crate-level `#![allow(dead_code)]` — standard idiom for
  `tests/common/`: each `tests/*.rs` compiles as its own crate, so
  helpers used by some test crates but not others would otherwise
  warn.

**New test files:**

- `tests/cli_smoke.rs` (2 tests):
  - `cli_version_runs` — `vc-x1 --version` exits 0 and stdout
    contains `"vc-x1"`. Pins `CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1` resolves and
    the binary actually runs.
  - `cli_help_lists_init` — `vc-x1 --help` lists the `init`
    subcommand. Pins clap's subcommand surface compiled in.
- `tests/cli_init.rs` (2 tests, counterparts to in-process
  fixtures):
  - `cli_init_por_creates_layout` — mirrors
    `por_fixture_creates_single_repo_layout`.
  - `cli_init_dual_creates_layout` — mirrors
    `dual_fixture_creates_dual_repo_layout`.

**Tempdir-root override (`$VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR`):**

Both `unique_base` helpers (in-process and CLI) resolve the parent
directory in priority order:

1. `$VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR` (if set and non-empty).
2. `std::env::temp_dir()` (= `$TMPDIR` on Unix, else `/tmp`).

Useful for steering tests onto a tmpfs / SSD / project-local path
without exporting `TMPDIR` globally. Future work (per
`notes/todo.md`) extends the chain through
`~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` and project-local
`.vc-config.toml`.

**Shared `resolve_tmp_root` via `#[path]`:**

`vc-x1` is binary-only (no `lib.rs`), so the integration-test
crate can't import from `src/test_helpers.rs` (which is
`#[cfg(test)]`-gated inside `main.rs`). To avoid duplicating the
~10-line resolver, lifted it into `src/test_tmp_root.rs` (pure
stdlib) and reach it from two contexts:

- `src/main.rs` — `#[cfg(test)] mod test_tmp_root;` (sibling of
  `test_helpers`); `src/test_helpers.rs` then
  `use crate::test_tmp_root::resolve_tmp_root;`.
- `tests/common/mod.rs` —
  `#[path = "../../src/test_tmp_root.rs"] mod test_tmp_root;` +
  `use test_tmp_root::resolve_tmp_root;`.

Two separate compilations of the same source — no cross-crate
linking. Constraint maintained: the file is dependency-free
(no `crate::*` imports), so the integration-test crate (whose
crate root is `tests/cli_init.rs`, not `src/main.rs`) can compile
it. When the priority chain extends to config files, the
config-loading should pass its result *in*, preserving this
constraint.

The user framed this as a precedent: **strive to share code, not
mandatory but a "good to have"**. The duplication threshold for
applying `#[path]` is judgment-driven; here, ~10 lines × 2 sites
+ expected growth made it worthwhile.

**README.md `## Testing` section:**

New top-level section before `## Contributing`. Documents:

- The two flavors (in-process vs CLI subprocess) and how to run
  each (`cargo test`, `--lib`, `--test cli_init`).
- The `$VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR` env var with example usage and the
  cleanup `find` recipe for SIGKILL leaks.
- Pointer to the todo for the future config-file extension.

**Test counts:**

- 327 in-process tests (unit + library), unchanged.
- 2 `cli_smoke` integration tests (new).
- 2 `cli_init` integration tests (new).
- Total: 331, all green.

**WIP ladder:**

Single commit; harness + smoke + init tests + tempdir-root sharing
+ README docs + todo entry all ride together. The harness alone
isn't useful without tests; the env-var support isn't useful
without doc; sharing the resolver with the in-process side is the
new precedent the user wanted to establish from day one.

### Decisions made during design

content.text:
That's correct behavior, not a bug. RAII `Drop` removes the tempdir when the fixture goes out of scope at test end:

- `tests/common/mod.rs:120-122` — `impl Drop for CliFixture` runs `remove_dir_all(&self.base)`.
- `src/test_helpers.rs` — `impl Drop for Fixture` and `impl Drop for FixturePor` do the same.

A successful test leaves no residue in `$TMPDIR` by design — that's why the README's cleanup recipe targets `-mtime +1` (only stale leaks from SIGKILLed runs survive).

Quick proof they exist *while* the test is running:

content.text:
Captured: the tempdir `/tmp/vc-x1-cli-test-init-dual-layout-…` exists during the test (caught around iteration 4 ≈ 200ms in), and is gone after the test exits. So Drop is working as designed.

If you want to inspect tempdirs after a test for debugging, options:
1. **Quickest hack** — comment out the `let _ = std::fs::remove_dir_all(...)` line in the relevant `Drop` impl temporarily.
2. **Cleaner knob (small future enhancement)** — wire a `$VC_X1_TEST_KEEP` env var that suppresses `remove_dir_all` when set. Would land in both `Drop` impls; ~6 lines. Could fit in the same future cycle as the config-extension todo, or as its own one-liner.
3. **Failed test** — failing assertions panic before `Drop`'s ordinary path? No, actually `Drop` still runs on panic during stack unwind, so failed tests *also* clean up. To preserve a failing test's tempdir for inspection, you'd need option 1 or 2.

Want me to add `$VC_X1_TEST_KEEP` support now (small, useful for the iteration phase you mentioned), or capture it as a todo for later?

content.content: The file /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/src/test_tmp_root.rs has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: /// Pairs with `--nocapture` so the per-fixture stderr line
/// announcing the preserved path is visible.
+/// Pure-policy form of `should_keep_tempdir`. Takes the env-var
+/// value (`None` if unset, `Some("")` if set-but-empty) and
+/// returns whether to preserve. Factored out so the decision is
+/// unit-testable without manipulating global env state — env
+/// mutation is not thread-safe and would race with other tests
+    /// "set but explicitly disabled" form (matches how env vars
+    /// are unset on some shells via `VAR=`).
+    /// Any non-empty value → preserve. Aligns with the conventional
+    /// or even `VAR=0`, since the policy treats *any* non-empty

content.text:
Two things going on, both shell-mechanical:

1. **`VC_X1_TEST_KEEP` wasn't set in your environment.** The `eprintln!` only fires when the env var is non-empty. Without it, `Drop` just deletes as before — silent.

2. **`eprintln!` goes to stderr, but `|` only pipes stdout.** Even if the env var were set, `rg` would never see the message because the pipe drops stderr.

The full incantation:

```bash
VC_X1_TEST_KEEP=1 cargo test -- --nocapture 2>&1 | rg TEST_KEEP
```

file.content:
## Testing

The repo ships two flavors of tests:

- **In-process tests** — `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { … }` blocks inside
  `src/*.rs`. These call library code directly (no subprocess spawn)
  and run fastest. The dual / POR fixture tests under
  `src/init.rs::tests` build throwaway workspaces by invoking
  `init::init_with_symlink` as a function.
- **CLI subprocess integration tests** — files under `tests/`. These
  spawn the `vc-x1` binary that Cargo built (via
  `env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1")`) so the full argument-parsing,
  exit-code, and stdout/stderr surface is exercised. Each subprocess
  gets a `HOME` override so the user's real `~/.config/vc-x1/` can't
  leak in or get clobbered.

Run everything with:

```bash
cargo test                 # unit + integration
cargo test --lib           # in-process only
cargo test --test cli_init # one integration test crate
```

### Test tempdir location

Both test layers create throwaway fixtures under a tempdir. The
parent directory resolves in priority order:

1. `$VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR` — explicit env override.
2. `std::env::temp_dir()` — standard fallback (`$TMPDIR` on Unix,
   else `/tmp`).

Useful when you want tests on a tmpfs / SSD / project-local path
without exporting `TMPDIR` globally:

```bash
VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR=/dev/shm/vc-x1 cargo test
```

Fixture directories are named `vc-x1-test-<tag>-<ts>-<n>` (in-process)
and `vc-x1-cli-test-<tag>-<ts>-<n>` (CLI). RAII drop removes them on
test exit; SIGKILLs / panics in `Drop` can leak — search and clean
manually:

```bash
find "${VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR:-${TMPDIR:-/tmp}}" -maxdepth 1 \
     -name 'vc-x1-*test*' -mtime +1 -exec rm -rf {} +
```

A future enhancement (tracked in `notes/todo.md`) extends the
priority chain through `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` and project-local
`.vc-config.toml`.

## Contributing

## Contributing
input.new_string:
A future enhancement (tracked in `notes/todo.md`) extends the
priority chain through `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` and project-local
`.vc-config.toml`.

### Preserving fixtures for debugging

When a test fails and you want to inspect the workspace it built,
set `$VC_X1_TEST_KEEP`. The RAII `Drop` impls (`Fixture`,
`FixturePor`, `CliFixture`) skip `remove_dir_all` and print the
preserved path on stderr:

```bash
VC_X1_TEST_KEEP=1 cargo test -- --nocapture 2>&1 | grep TEST_KEEP
```

Two shell gotchas worth remembering:

- The preservation message goes to **stderr**, so a plain
  `cargo test | grep ...` won't see it — use `2>&1 |` or write
  the full output to a file.
- `--nocapture` is needed to bypass libtest's stdout/stderr
  capture; without it, the messages get swallowed by the test
  runner's pretty-printer.

`VC_X1_TEST_KEEP` is a debugging knob — every fixture-creating
test in the run leaks its tempdir while it's set. Clean up with
the `find` recipe above, or just `rm -rf` the announced paths.

toolUseResult.newString:
A future enhancement (tracked in `notes/todo.md`) extends the
priority chain through `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` and project-local
`.vc-config.toml`.

### Preserving fixtures for debugging

When a test fails and you want to inspect the workspace it built,
set `$VC_X1_TEST_KEEP`. The RAII `Drop` impls (`Fixture`,
`FixturePor`, `CliFixture`) skip `remove_dir_all` and print the
preserved path on stderr:

```bash
VC_X1_TEST_KEEP=1 cargo test -- --nocapture 2>&1 | grep TEST_KEEP
```

Two shell gotchas worth remembering:

- The preservation message goes to **stderr**, so a plain
  `cargo test | grep ...` won't see it — use `2>&1 |` or write
  the full output to a file.
- `--nocapture` is needed to bypass libtest's stdout/stderr
  capture; without it, the messages get swallowed by the test
  runner's pretty-printer.

`VC_X1_TEST_KEEP` is a debugging knob — every fixture-creating
test in the run leaks its tempdir while it's set. Clean up with
the `find` recipe above, or just `rm -rf` the announced paths.

## Contributing

content.text:
Down to just `/tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log` (a non-fixture log). Decision point:

**The `cfg_tempdir` leak is unrelated to -6.4 in scope**, but it's directly relevant in spirit — we just made tempdir handling a first-class concern with `resolve_tmp_root()` + `should_keep_tempdir()` + RAII `Drop`. Letting one module leak is inconsistent with the precedent.

**Options:**

1. **Fix in -6.4** — replace `cfg_tempdir() -> PathBuf` with a small RAII `CfgTempDir(PathBuf)` struct that:
   - Builds via `resolve_tmp_root().join(format!("vc-x1-cfg-{tag}-{ts}-{n}"))` (now matches the in-process / CLI naming pattern: `vc-x1-cfg-<tag>-<ts>-<n>`)
   - Honors `should_keep_tempdir()` in `Drop`
   - `Deref<Target = Path>` so existing call sites still work
   
   ~20 lines, all in src/config.rs::tests. Brings config tests under the shared infra. Adds 1 unit test asserting Drop cleans up.

2. **Defer** — capture as a todo, keep -6.4 focused on the harness + initial CLI tests it shipped.

My lean: option 1, since (a) it's small, (b) it generalizes the precedent the user explicitly named ("strive to share code"), and (c) catching this leak right after building the `should_keep_tempdir` knob is the natural moment.

Which way?

input.new_string:
**Preserve-on-drop knob (`$VC_X1_TEST_KEEP`):**

A second env var, also resolved through `src/test_tmp_root.rs`,
suppresses RAII tempdir cleanup for debugging. When set
(non-empty), each fixture's `Drop` skips `remove_dir_all` and
prints `VC_X1_TEST_KEEP set; preserving <path>` on stderr.

```bash
VC_X1_TEST_KEEP=1 cargo test -- --nocapture 2>&1 | grep TEST_KEEP
```

(Both `2>&1` and `--nocapture` are required — `eprintln!` goes
to stderr; libtest captures stdout/stderr by default.)

Implementation:

- `src/test_tmp_root.rs` exposes `pub fn should_keep_tempdir()`
  alongside `resolve_tmp_root`. Pure-policy form `keep_decision`
  is factored out (takes `Option<&str>`) so the env-var-reading
  wrapper isn't called from unit tests — env mutation isn't
  thread-safe and would race with parallel `Drop`s.
- 3 unit tests on `keep_decision` (`unset`, `empty`, `nonempty`)
  pin the policy without touching `$VC_X1_TEST_KEEP`.
- All three RAII `Drop` impls (`Fixture`, `FixturePor`,
  `CliFixture`) consult `should_keep_tempdir()`. Same pattern in
  all three: skip removal + `eprintln!` when keep, otherwise
  best-effort `remove_dir_all`.
- New `tests/cli_keep.rs` (single-test binary) exercises the
  env-var path end-to-end: sets `VC_X1_TEST_KEEP=1` (`unsafe`
  per Rust 1.83+), creates a `CliFixture`, drops it, asserts
  the tempdir survives, manually removes, restores env. Lives
  in its own test crate so the env-write doesn't race with
  parallel reads in `cli_init` / `cli_smoke` (separate
  processes, no shared env).

**`cfg_tempdir` leak fix (`src/config.rs::tests`):**

`config.rs`'s test module had a long-standing leak: a private
`cfg_tempdir(tag) -> PathBuf` helper created
`/tmp/vc-x1-cfg-<tag>-<ts>/` and never cleaned up — every test
invocation that called it (8 tests) leaked one dir. After many
local `cargo test` runs, the user's `/tmp` had ~217 stale
`vc-x1-cfg-*` dirs.

Fixed by replacing the bare `PathBuf` helper with a `CfgTempDir`
RAII struct:

- Same shape as the other fixtures (counter + `resolve_tmp_root`
  + `should_keep_tempdir` in `Drop`).
- New naming convention `vc-x1-cfg-<tag>-<ts>-<n>` aligns with
  `vc-x1-test-*` and `vc-x1-cli-test-*`.
- `write_cfg(tag, contents)` now returns `(CfgTempDir, PathBuf)`
  — caller binds the guard to a `_cfg` slot to keep it alive
  for the test scope; existing 6 call sites updated 1:1.
- New unit test `cfg_tempdir_drop_cleans_up` pins the cleanup
  invariant: capture path, drop guard, assert path is gone.

This isn't strictly part of the -6.4 brief, but the cycle is
specifically about tempdir hygiene and the leak surfaced while
verifying `should_keep_tempdir` worked. Bringing config tests
under the shared infra (rather than letting one module quietly
leak) keeps the new precedent honest.

**README.md `## Testing` section:**

New top-level section before `## Contributing`. Documents:

- The two flavors (in-process vs CLI subprocess) and how to run
  each (`cargo test`, `--bins`, `--test cli_init`).
- The `$VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR` env var with example usage and the
  cleanup `find` recipe for SIGKILL leaks.
- The `$VC_X1_TEST_KEEP` env var with the full
  `2>&1 | grep`/`--nocapture` incantation and the two shell
  gotchas (stderr vs stdout, libtest capture).
- Pointer to the todo for the future config-file extension.

**Test counts:**

- 331 binary unit tests (327 prior + 3 `keep_decision` +
  1 `cfg_tempdir_drop_cleans_up`).
- 5 `cli_init` (2 init tests + 3 `keep_decision` via
  `#[path]`).
- 4 `cli_keep` (1 keep-fixture test + 3 `keep_decision` via
  `#[path]`).
- 5 `cli_smoke` (2 smoke tests + 3 `keep_decision` via
  `#[path]`).
- Total: 345, all green. `keep_decision` runs in 4 contexts
  due to the `#[path]` include; each run is microseconds and
  also confirms the shared file compiles cleanly in every
  test crate.

**WIP ladder:**

Single commit. Six concerns ride together because they form
one coherent story about tempdir hygiene:

1. CLI subprocess harness (`tests/common/`).
2. First subprocess test files (`cli_smoke`, `cli_init`).
3. `$VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR` resolution + shared `resolve_tmp_root`
   via `#[path]` — the precedent the user wanted to establish.
4. `$VC_X1_TEST_KEEP` knob + Drop wiring across all three
   fixtures + `cli_keep` end-to-end test.
5. `cfg_tempdir` leak fix using the same shared infra.
6. README + todo + chores docs.

The harness alone isn't useful without tests; the env-var
support isn't useful without docs; sharing the resolver and
keep-decision with the in-process side is the precedent
("strive to share, not mandatory but a good to have"). Fixing
`cfg_tempdir` while we're already in the area aligns the rest
of the codebase with that precedent.

content.text:
All gates green. Proposed commit message for `vc-x1 push init-clone-refactor`:

**Title:**
```
test: CLI subprocess tests + tempdir hygiene (0.41.1-6.4)
```

**Body:**
```
First true CLI integration tests + tempdir-handling cleanup.
tests/ crate spawns the vc-x1 binary via env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1");
new $VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR (root override) and $VC_X1_TEST_KEEP
(skip-drop debug knob) are shared between in-process and
integration tests via #[path]. Fixed a long-standing cfg_tempdir
leak. Suite: 331 + 5 + 4 + 5 = 345.

- Cargo.toml: bump to 0.41.1-6.4
- src/test_tmp_root.rs (new): resolve_tmp_root +
  should_keep_tempdir + keep_decision + 3 unit tests
- src/main.rs: declare #[cfg(test)] mod test_tmp_root
- src/test_helpers.rs: import shared resolvers; Drop honors
  should_keep_tempdir on Fixture + FixturePor
- src/config.rs: replace leaking cfg_tempdir with CfgTempDir
  RAII; new cfg_tempdir_drop_cleans_up test
- tests/common/mod.rs (new): vc_x1(), run_ok/_err, CliFixture
  (HOME isolation, Drop honors should_keep_tempdir);
  #[path]-include of test_tmp_root
- tests/cli_smoke.rs (new): --version + --help (2 tests)
- tests/cli_init.rs (new): POR + dual layout (2 tests)
- tests/cli_keep.rs (new): VC_X1_TEST_KEEP preserves fixture
  (1 test, single-test binary for env-write isolation)
- README.md: new ## Testing section (subprocess, tempdir,
  preserve-on-drop)
- notes/todo.md: -6.4 (done); add tempdir-override-chain item
- notes/chores-08.md: new -6.4 subsection
```

OK to run `vc-x1 push init-clone-refactor`?

message.content: explain env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1") seems an odd env name

content.text:
It's a Cargo-specific env var, set automatically by Cargo at **compile time** for integration tests under `tests/`. Quick anatomy:

**Naming:** `CARGO_BIN_EXE_<binary-name>` — the suffix is the literal name of a binary target from `Cargo.toml`. For us that's `vc-x1`, so the var is `CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1` (yes, with the hyphen — Cargo doesn't sanitize).

**When it's set:** only when compiling files under `tests/` (Cargo's "integration test" target type). Not in `src/`, not in `examples/`, not in build scripts. Each binary in the package gets its own `CARGO_BIN_EXE_*` var pointing at where Cargo built that binary for the current test profile.

**Value:** an absolute path to the compiled binary, like `/home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/target/debug/vc-x1`. Cargo guarantees the binary is built before the test runs, so the path is always valid by the time `env!` evaluates.

**Why `env!` and not `std::env::var`:**
- `env!("FOO")` is a macro that reads `FOO` at **compile time** — the value is baked into the test binary as a string literal. Compile fails if `FOO` is unset.
- `std::env::var("FOO")` is a runtime function — reads whatever's in the process env when called.

For locating the binary, compile-time is correct: the path won't change between compile and run, and we want a hard error if the test target accidentally moves out of the right context (e.g., someone tries to run the harness from `src/`).

**The "odd" feel:** Cargo invents a few env vars like this (`CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR`, `CARGO_PKG_VERSION`, `CARGO_TARGET_TMPDIR`, etc.). They look like environment variables but in practice they're a *protocol* between Cargo and your code, set only when Cargo invokes your build/test. The integration-test pattern of `Command::new(env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1"))` is the documented Rust idiom — see [The Cargo Book → Environment variables Cargo sets for crates](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/environment-variables.html#environment-variables-cargo-sets-for-crates).

Tradeoff vs alternatives:
- Hard-coding `target/debug/vc-x1` would break in `--release` and on cross-compile.
- Using `which("vc-x1")` would find the system-installed copy (potentially stale), not the freshly-built one.
- `env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1")` is the only one that says "the version Cargo just built."

Good catch — the test body doesn't show `env!` directly because the indirection is one layer up. Trace:

```
cli_version_runs (tests/cli_smoke.rs)
  └─ fx.cmd()                    ← CliFixture::cmd
       └─ vc_x1()                ← common::vc_x1 (tests/common/mod.rs:47)
            └─ Command::new(env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1"))
```

So the test calls `fx.cmd()` → `cmd()` adds the HOME env override and returns the `Command` already pre-loaded with the binary path from `vc_x1()`, which is the only place `env!` is invoked.

**The doc comment is technically correct but reads as a non-sequitur** — a reader looking at just `cli_version_runs` sees `fx.cmd()` and `--version`, no `env!` anywhere. The link isn't visible without chasing two helpers up the call stack.

Two ways to fix:

**(a) Restate the test's actual surface, drop the `env!` reference:**

```rust
/// `vc-x1 --version` exits 0 and prints a line containing the
/// crate name. Smoke check that the harness can spawn the binary
/// Cargo built and the binary runs to completion.
```

**(b) Make the indirection explicit:**

```rust
/// `vc-x1 --version` exits 0 and prints a line containing the
/// crate name. Smoke check that `CliFixture::cmd()` →

lastPrompt: I tweaked the "dead_code" and why do we have to add the allow(dead_code)?

aiTitle: Review workflow and file approval process

content.text:
Each `tests/*.rs` is compiled as its **own separate test crate**, and each one declares `mod common;` which pulls in **everything** from `tests/common/mod.rs`. But individual test crates only use a *subset* of the helpers.

Concrete example with current code:

| Helper | `cli_smoke` | `cli_init` | `cli_keep` |
|---|---|---|---|
| `CliFixture::new` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| `CliFixture::cmd` | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| `CliFixture::path` | — | ✓ | — |
| `run_ok` | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| `run_err` | — | — | — |

`run_err` is dead in **all three** test crates today. `cmd` is dead in `cli_keep`. `path` is dead in `cli_smoke` and `cli_keep`. And so on.

Without `#![allow(dead_code)]`, each test crate's compilation would emit warnings for the helpers it doesn't reach — three different warning sets, depending on which crate is compiling. The warnings would be noise, not signal: the helpers exist for *future* tests in *other* test crates that haven't been written yet (`run_err` is the obvious one — first error-path test that needs it will use it).

**Why the standard fix is crate-level allow on `common/mod.rs`:**
- Per-item `#[allow(dead_code)]` annotations would have to live on every helper that's dead in any crate — and the set changes as tests get added/removed.
- You can't conditionally allow based on which test crate is compiling — there's no cfg flag for "I'm being included by `cli_smoke`."
- Refactoring to "only include what each crate uses" defeats the purpose of having a shared module.

So the crate-level `#![allow(dead_code)]` at the top of the shared module is the documented Rust idiom for `tests/common/`. It says "this module's contents are intentionally a superset of any one crate's needs; don't warn about that."

The trade-off you accept: the allow also suppresses warnings about *genuinely* dead helpers (ones no test crate will ever use). For a small shared module this is fine — periodic manual review catches accumulated cruft. If the module grows large enough that this becomes risky, the standard escalation is to split helpers into multiple smaller `tests/common/<topic>.rs` files, each gated to where it's actually needed.

content.text: Yes, that works — both `//!` and `#![…]` are "inner" pre-item things, so the allow can sit right above the first item (the `use` statements) as long as no items come before it. The two `//!` blocks merge into one contiguous comment, then the attribute, then `use`.

720	## Testing
721	
722	The repo ships two flavors of tests:
723	
724	- **In-process tests** — `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { … }` blocks inside
725	  `src/*.rs`. These call library code directly (no subprocess spawn)
726	  and run fastest. The dual / POR fixture tests under
727	  `src/init.rs::tests` build throwaway workspaces by invoking
728	  `init::init_with_symlink` as a function.
729	- **CLI subprocess integration tests** — files under `tests/`. These
730	  spawn the `vc-x1` binary that Cargo built (via
731	  `env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1")`) so the full argument-parsing,
732	  exit-code, and stdout/stderr surface is exercised. Each subprocess
733	  gets a `HOME` override so the user's real `~/.config/vc-x1/` can't
734	  leak in or get clobbered.
735	
736	Run everything with:
737	
738	```bash
739	cargo test                 # unit + integration
740	cargo test --bins          # binary unit tests only (in-process)
741	cargo test --test cli_init # one integration test crate
742	```
743	
744	### Test tempdir location
745	
746	Both test layers create throwaway fixtures under a tempdir. The
747	parent directory resolves in priority order:
748	
749	1. `$VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR` — explicit env override.
750	2. `std::env::temp_dir()` — standard fallback (`$TMPDIR` on Unix,
751	   else `/tmp`).
752	
753	Useful when you want tests on a tmpfs / SSD / project-local path
754	without exporting `TMPDIR` globally:
755	
756	```bash
757	VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR=/dev/shm/vc-x1 cargo test
758	```
759	
760	Fixture directories are named `vc-x1-test-<tag>-<ts>-<n>` (in-process)
761	and `vc-x1-cli-test-<tag>-<ts>-<n>` (CLI). RAII drop removes them on
762	test exit; SIGKILLs / panics in `Drop` can leak — search and clean
763	manually:
764	
765	```bash
766	find "${VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR:-${TMPDIR:-/tmp}}" -maxdepth 1 \
767	     -name 'vc-x1-*test*' -mtime +1 -exec rm -rf {} +
768	```
769	
770	A future enhancement (tracked in `notes/todo.md`) extends the
771	priority chain through `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` and project-local
772	`.vc-config.toml`.
773	
774	### Preserving fixtures for debugging
775	
776	When a test fails and you want to inspect the workspace it built,
777	set `$VC_X1_TEST_KEEP`. The RAII `Drop` impls (`Fixture`,
778	`FixturePor`, `CliFixture`) skip `remove_dir_all` and print the
779	preserved path on stderr:
780	
781	```bash
782	VC_X1_TEST_KEEP=1 cargo test -- --nocapture 2>&1 | grep TEST_KEEP
783	```
784	
785	Two shell gotchas worth remembering:
786	
787	- The preservation message goes to **stderr**, so a plain
788	  `cargo test | grep ...` won't see it — use `2>&1 |` or write
789	  the full output to a file.
790	- `--nocapture` is needed to bypass libtest's stdout/stderr
791	  capture; without it, the messages get swallowed by the test
792	  runner's pretty-printer.
793	
794	`VC_X1_TEST_KEEP` is a debugging knob — every fixture-creating
795	test in the run leaks its tempdir while it's set. Clean up with
796	the `find` recipe above, or just `rm -rf` the announced paths.
797	
798	## Contributing
799

801	canonical in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md):
802	
803	- [Versioning during development](CLAUDE.md#versioning) — `-N`
804	  pre-release suffix convention (single-step vs multi-step).
805	- [Commit message style](CLAUDE.md#commit-message-style).
806	- [Commit-Push-Finalize Flow](CLAUDE.md#commit-push-finalize-flow) —
807	  two-checkpoint per-step discipline.
808	- [Code Conventions](CLAUDE.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
809	  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,

file.content:
## Testing

The repo ships two flavors of tests:

- **In-process tests** — `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { … }` blocks inside
  `src/*.rs`. These call library code directly (no subprocess spawn)
  and run fastest. The dual / POR fixture tests under
  `src/init.rs::tests` build throwaway workspaces by invoking
  `init::init_with_symlink` as a function.
- **CLI subprocess integration tests** — files under `tests/`. These
  spawn the `vc-x1` binary that Cargo built (via
  `env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1")`) so the full argument-parsing,
  exit-code, and stdout/stderr surface is exercised. Each subprocess
  gets a `HOME` override so the user's real `~/.config/vc-x1/` can't
  leak in or get clobbered.

Run everything with:

```bash
cargo test                 # unit + integration
cargo test --bins          # binary unit tests only (in-process)
cargo test --test cli_init # one integration test crate
```

### Test tempdir location

Both test layers create throwaway fixtures under a tempdir. The
parent directory resolves in priority order:

1. `$VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR` — explicit env override.
2. `std::env::temp_dir()` — standard fallback (`$TMPDIR` on Unix,
   else `/tmp`).

Useful when you want tests on a tmpfs / SSD / project-local path
without exporting `TMPDIR` globally:

```bash
VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR=/dev/shm/vc-x1 cargo test
```

Fixture directories are named `vc-x1-test-<tag>-<ts>-<n>` (in-process)
and `vc-x1-cli-test-<tag>-<ts>-<n>` (CLI). RAII drop removes them on
test exit; SIGKILLs / panics in `Drop` can leak — search and clean
manually:

```bash
find "${VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR:-${TMPDIR:-/tmp}}" -maxdepth 1 \
     -name 'vc-x1-*test*' -mtime +1 -exec rm -rf {} +
```

A future enhancement (tracked in `notes/todo.md`) extends the
priority chain through `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` and project-local
`.vc-config.toml`.

### Preserving fixtures for debugging

When a test fails and you want to inspect the workspace it built,
set `$VC_X1_TEST_KEEP`. The RAII `Drop` impls (`Fixture`,
`FixturePor`, `CliFixture`) skip `remove_dir_all` and print the
preserved path on stderr:

```bash
VC_X1_TEST_KEEP=1 cargo test -- --nocapture 2>&1 | grep TEST_KEEP
```

Two shell gotchas worth remembering:

- The preservation message goes to **stderr**, so a plain
  `cargo test | grep ...` won't see it — use `2>&1 |` or write
  the full output to a file.
- `--nocapture` is needed to bypass libtest's stdout/stderr
  capture; without it, the messages get swallowed by the test
  runner's pretty-printer.

`VC_X1_TEST_KEEP` is a debugging knob — every fixture-creating
test in the run leaks its tempdir while it's set. Clean up with
the `find` recipe above, or just `rm -rf` the announced paths.

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md):

- [Versioning during development](CLAUDE.md#versioning) — `-N`
  pre-release suffix convention (single-step vs multi-step).
- [Commit message style](CLAUDE.md#commit-message-style).
- [Commit-Push-Finalize Flow](CLAUDE.md#commit-push-finalize-flow) —
  two-checkpoint per-step discipline.
- [Code Conventions](CLAUDE.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,

content.text:
I can't see exactly which tokens you changed (jj/git both show the section as wholly new vs HEAD). Here's an honest review of the bullet as it currently stands:

```
- **CLI subprocess integration tests** — files under `tests/`. These
  spawn the `vc-x1` binary that Cargo built (via
  `env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1")`) so the full argument-parsing,
  exit-code, and stdout/stderr surface is exercised. Each subprocess
  gets a `HOME` override so the user's real `~/.config/vc-x1/` can't
  leak in or get clobbered.
```

**Reads well.** Conveys what the tests are, how the binary is located, what's being exercised, and why HOME is overridden. No factual issues.

**Possible nits** (take or leave):

1. *"is exercised"* — the subject "argument-parsing, exit-code, and stdout/stderr surface" is awkward as a singular. Tighter:
   > so argument parsing, exit codes, and stdout/stderr are all exercised end-to-end.

2. *"files under `tests/`"* — true but understates the structure. A reader new to Rust testing might not know each `tests/*.rs` is its own crate. Optional:
   > each `tests/*.rs` is compiled as a standalone test crate that spawns the `vc-x1` binary…

3. *Doesn't list which test crates currently exist* (`cli_smoke`, `cli_init`, `cli_keep`). The "Run everything with" block has `--test cli_init` as an example but doesn't enumerate. Could add a short list, or leave to discovery — your call. Not stale-prone if kept short.

4. *`env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1")`* is a useful breadcrumb but reads slightly impl-detail-heavy for a README. I'd keep it because it gives readers a search term to find where the wiring lives.

**Spotted no errors.** If you want, I can apply nit (1) — the only one that's a small unambiguous improvement. The others are judgment calls.

content.text:
Read carefully. Two things:

**1. Typo (worth fixing):** `binaries location` → `binary's location` (possessive apostrophe). "binaries" without `'s` is a plural noun, which doesn't fit here.

**2. Optional tightening:** *"the value in `env!(...)`"* — `env!` is a macro, not a container, so "in" reads slightly off. More natural: *"the value of `env!(...)`"* or *"the path that `env!(...)` returns"*.

Tightened version that keeps your intent (explicit, non-parenthetical):

```markdown
- **CLI subprocess integration tests** — files under `tests/`. These
  spawn the `vc-x1` binary that Cargo built; the binary's location
  is the value of `env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1")`. The full
  argument-parsing, exit-code, and stdout/stderr surface is
  exercised. Each subprocess gets a `HOME` override so the user's
  real `~/.config/vc-x1/` can't leak in or get clobbered.
```

Two small structural changes vs your edit:
- `built and the binaries location is` → `built; the binary's location is` — semicolon breaks the run-on sentence cleanly.
- `env!(...) so the full ...` → `env!(...). The full ...` — splits into two sentences so the grammar of the second one stands alone (and lets you read it without backtracking).

Apart from the typo, the original is clear enough — only apply the structural changes if you like them.

message.content: It's "in" the environment variable returned by a macro it's didn't fabricate it, some macro's create things so being explicit the source of truth is the environment variable, correct?

content.text:
You're right and I was being too rigid about "in" — semantically the source of truth IS the environment variable; the macro is just the read mechanism. Your "in" carries that meaning intentionally.

The reason "in `env!(...)`" reads slightly off is purely shape: `env!(...)` is *call notation* (a macro invocation), not the env var itself, so "in" applied to a function-call-looking thing feels off. But the *meaning* is right.

Two ways to keep your intent and dodge the shape issue:

**(a) Name the env var directly, mention `env!` as the access mechanism:**
```markdown
the binary's location is the value of the `CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1`
env var, which `env!()` reads at compile time
```

**(b) Lean into "in":**
```markdown
the binary's location is in the env var read by
`env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1")`
```

(a) makes the env-var-as-source-of-truth point loudest. (b) is closer to your phrasing.

If you do want to keep your version more or less as-is, the only must-fix is the typo:
> `binaries location` → `binary's location`

The "in" debate is a stylistic call, not a correctness one. You picked it deliberately to convey "env var is the real source," which is true.

message.content: It reads it at runtime or compile time, I would guess it variable is read at runtime passing the contents as the executable to run?

content.text:
Compile time, not runtime. This is the core distinction between the two `env`-named things in Rust:

| | When it reads | If unset |
|---|---|---|
| `env!("FOO")` (macro) | At **compile time** | Compile error |
| `std::env::var("FOO")` (function) | At **runtime** | Returns `Err` |

For `env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1")`:

1. When Cargo builds the test binary (e.g. `tests/cli_smoke.rs`), Cargo sets `CARGO_BIN_EXE_vc-x1=/path/to/target/debug/vc-x1` in the *compiler's* environment.
2. `rustc` expands the `env!` macro, reads that env var, and **substitutes the value as a string literal** into the generated code.
3. The compiled test binary has the literal path baked in as a `&'static str`. There's no env var lookup at runtime — the path is just sitting in the data segment.
4. When `cargo test` runs the binary, `Command::new("/path/to/target/debug/vc-x1")` uses the already-embedded string.

**Why `env!` instead of `std::env::var`** for this use case:
- **Compile-time guarantee** — if the build context isn't right (e.g., someone tries to build the test outside Cargo), it fails at compile time with a clear error, rather than at runtime with a missing-env-var panic.
- **No runtime cost** — no env lookup, no `String` allocation; just a constant `&str`.
- **Standard idiom** — Cargo documents this as the canonical pattern for integration tests locating a built binary.

If you want to verify, you can disassemble or `strings` the test binary — the path will be visible as a literal:

```bash
strings target/debug/deps/cli_smoke-* | grep target/debug/vc-x1
```

It's compiled in, not looked up. So when the README says "the binary's location is in the env var read by `env!(...)`," **the reading happens during compilation; the runtime value is the embedded result.**
